justification: CMU is widely regarded as the top software engineering research institution in the world. It houses the Software Engineering Institute (SEI), the Institute for Software Research (ISR), and has produced foundational work in software architecture, formal methods, program analysis, testing, and human aspects of SE. Faculty such as Jonathan Aldrich, Claire Le Goues, Brad Myers, and others lead highly cited research programs.

MIT CSAIL hosts influential research in program synthesis, formal verification, software security, and programming languages. Work from groups led by faculty like Armando Solar-Lezama (program synthesis), Martin Rinard, and Michael Ernst has had major impact on SE research and practice.

Berkeley has strong SE research in program analysis, software security, programming languages, and distributed systems software. Faculty including Koushik Sen (fuzzing, testing), Dawn Song (security), and others contribute heavily to top SE venues.

UIUC has a long tradition of excellence in programming languages, compilers, program analysis, and software testing. Faculty such as Darko Marinov (software testing), Tao Xie, and Vikram Adve have produced highly influential SE research.

UW has strong research in programming languages, software engineering, HCI aspects of SE, and formal methods. Faculty including Michael Ernst (invariant detection, testing), Emina Torlak (program synthesis/verification), and René Just contribute significantly to SE research.

Georgia Tech has a strong SE research community with work in software testing, program repair, software analytics, and AI for SE. Faculty such as Alessandro Orso, Baishakhi Ray (now at Columbia but trained here), and others have made major contributions to ICSE, FSE, and ASE.

UT Austin has notable strength in program synthesis, formal verification, programming languages, and software security. Faculty including Işıl Dillig, Swarat Chaudhuri, and Miryung Kim contribute to top SE and PL venues.

Stanford contributes to SE through work in program analysis, software security, formal verification, and AI-assisted software development. Faculty such as Alex Aiken (program analysis) and Dawson Engler have produced landmark SE research.

Columbia has growing strength in SE research, particularly in software testing, program analysis, AI for SE, and software security. Faculty including Baishakhi Ray (AI for SE, code generation) and Junfeng Yang (systems and testing) are highly active.

UCLA's SEAL (Software Engineering and Analysis Lab) led by Miryung Kim (now at UCLA) and other faculty contributes to software evolution, program analysis, and empirical SE. The department has a solid presence at major SE conferences.

Purdue has active SE research in software testing, program analysis, software security, and empirical SE. Faculty contributions appear regularly at ICSE, FSE, and ASE, and the department has a strong graduate program in SE.

Michigan has notable SE research in program analysis, software reliability, and human aspects of software engineering. Faculty such as Westley Weimer (program repair, genetic improvement) contribute prominently to the SE research community.

UCSD contributes to SE through research in program synthesis, programming languages, software verification, and software security. Faculty including Ranjit Jhala and Nadia Polikarpova (program synthesis, type systems) are highly active in SE and PL venues.

NC State has a dedicated and productive SE research group with strengths in empirical SE, software analytics, mining software repositories, and AI for SE. Faculty such as Tim Menzies and Laurie Williams have high citation counts and strong presence at SE venues.

USC has a long history in software engineering research, particularly in software process, cost estimation, empirical SE, and software architecture. The CSSE center, historically associated with Barry Boehm, continues to produce relevant SE research.

CMU is widely regarded as the global leader in software engineering research. The SEI (a federally funded R&D center) and ISR produce foundational work in software architecture, formal methods, program analysis, security, and software process. Faculty such as David Garlan, Jonathan Aldrich, and Brad Myers have defined entire subfields. CMU consistently leads in PhD placements and citation impact in SE.

MIT's CSAIL houses world-class researchers in program synthesis, formal verification, programming languages, and software reliability. Work from groups led by figures like Armando Solar-Lezama (program synthesis) and Martin Rinard (program repair, fault tolerance) has had enormous influence on SE research.

Berkeley has long been a powerhouse in systems and software research. Contributions to software testing, program analysis, distributed systems software, and programming languages are substantial. Faculty like Koushik Sen (fuzzing, concolic testing) and Dawn Song (software security) are highly influential.

UIUC has exceptional depth in program analysis, software testing, formal verification, and programming languages. Faculty including Darko Marinov (software testing, regression testing), Tao Xie, and Vikram Adve have produced highly cited and impactful SE research.

UW's PLSE group is among the most productive in the country, with strengths in program synthesis, type systems, software correctness, and human aspects of SE. Faculty like Michael Ernst (invariant detection, type systems) and Emina Torlak (solver-aided programming) are field leaders.

Georgia Tech has strong SE research in automated program repair, software testing, mobile software, and empirical SE. Faculty such as Alessandro Orso (software testing, analysis) and Mary Jean Harrold (before her passing) have been central figures. The school has a large, active SE PhD program.

UT Austin is particularly strong in programming languages and formal methods as they intersect with SE, including program synthesis, verification, and software security. Isil Dillig and Işıl Dillig's group on program analysis and synthesis is internationally recognized.

Stanford contributes significantly to SE through work on software security, program analysis, bug finding, and developer tools. Faculty like Dawson Engler (static analysis, model checking) and Alex Aiken (program analysis) have produced landmark SE research and tools.

UCSD has notable strength in programming languages, software reliability, and empirical software engineering. Ranjit Jhala's work on liquid types and program verification, and work on software analytics, places UCSD among the top SE research institutions.

Columbia has strong SE research in software testing, program analysis, concurrency bugs, and software security. Junfeng Yang's work on finding bugs in systems software and Baishakhi Ray's work on AI for software engineering are highly cited.

Michigan has productive SE research in automated debugging, program repair, software testing, and empirical SE. Westley Weimer's work on automated program repair (GenProg) is among the most cited in the field. Strong connections between SE and systems research.

Purdue has consistent output in software security, program analysis, and software reliability. Aniket Kate and Xiangyu Zhang's work on dynamic analysis, provenance, and debugging has been influential. Strong industry connections through the Discovery Park.

USC's CSSE has a long tradition in empirical software engineering, software cost modeling (COCOMO), and software process research. Barry Boehm's legacy and ongoing work in software measurement, agile methods, and software economics give USC a distinctive niche.

NC State has a large and productive SE research group with strengths in empirical SE, mining software repositories, software testing, and developer productivity. Tim Menzies's work on AI/ML for SE and Laurie Williams's work on agile and security are well-known.

UMass Amherst has notable SE research in program analysis, software testing, and programming languages. Yuriy Brun's work on automated program repair and proactive SE, and work on distributed systems correctness, contributes to a solid SE research profile.

CMU is widely regarded as the top institution for software engineering research globally. The SEI, a federally funded R&D center, pioneered the Capability Maturity Model (CMM/CMMI). ISR faculty have made foundational contributions to software architecture, program analysis, formal methods, and empirical software engineering. CMU consistently produces top-cited SE researchers and PhD graduates who lead the field.

MIT CSAIL has produced landmark work in program synthesis, formal verification, model checking, and software correctness. Researchers such as Armando Solar-Lezama (program synthesis) and work on Alloy (Daniel Jackson) have had lasting influence. MIT's interdisciplinary environment drives high-impact SE research.

Berkeley has been central to distributed systems software, programming languages, and software reliability. Contributions include work on testing, verification, and systems software. Faculty like Dawn Song (security and program analysis) and others have shaped modern SE practice. Berkeley's influence on open-source software ecosystems is unmatched.

UIUC has a long tradition of excellence in compilers, program analysis, and software testing. Contributions include LLVM-related research, symbolic execution tools, and automated bug detection. Faculty such as Darko Marinov (software testing) and Tao Xie (empirical SE) have been highly influential.

Stanford has made major contributions to program analysis, software security, and AI-assisted software engineering. Work on static analysis tools (e.g., related to Coverity's academic origins), formal verification, and more recently LLM-based code generation has kept Stanford at the forefront of SE research.

UW's PLSE group is among the most active and cited in the world for programming languages and software engineering. Faculty including Michael Ernst (invariant detection, Daikon), Emina Torlak (Rosette, solver-aided languages), and René Just (mutation testing) have produced widely adopted tools and methods.

Georgia Tech has strong programs in software testing, program repair, and empirical software engineering. Faculty such as Alessandro Orso (program analysis and testing) and Myra Cohen have contributed heavily to testing research. Georgia Tech also has strong industry partnerships that amplify research impact.

UT Austin is known for foundational work in programming languages and formal methods with direct SE implications. Isil Dillig's work on program analysis and verification, and Işıl Dillig's group on automated reasoning, are highly cited. The department has produced influential tools and theoretical frameworks.

Columbia has contributed significantly to software testing, program analysis, and software reliability. Junfeng Yang's work on finding bugs in systems software and Baishakhi Ray's work on AI for software engineering are notable. Columbia's location in New York also facilitates strong industry collaboration.

UCSD has made important contributions to program analysis, software security, and programming languages. Ranjit Jhala's work on liquid types and software verification has been widely influential. The department has a strong record at top SE and PL venues.

Purdue has a strong tradition in software security, program analysis, and software engineering education research. CERIAS is one of the leading cybersecurity research centers, with significant overlap in secure software engineering. Faculty have contributed to vulnerability analysis and software hardening.

Michigan has produced influential work in software testing, program repair, and empirical software engineering. Westley Weimer's work on automated program repair (GenProg) is among the most cited in the field. The department has a strong presence at ICSE, FSE, and ASE.

USC's CSSE, historically associated with Barry Boehm's work on software cost estimation (COCOMO) and software process, has been a major contributor to empirical software engineering and software project management research. USC has a long legacy in SE research spanning decades.

NC State has a highly productive SE research group with strong output in mining software repositories, software analytics, and empirical SE. Tim Menzies's work on AI/ML applied to software engineering and Laurie Williams's work on agile and security have been widely cited.

UCI's ISR has a long history in software architecture, software processes, and collaborative software engineering. Richard Taylor and David Redmiles have contributed foundational work on software architecture and coordination. UCI's ISR is one of the oldest dedicated software research institutes in the US.

CMU has arguably the largest and most prolific computer vision research community in the world. Faculty such as Deva Ramanan, Abhinav Gupta, Martial Hebert, Ishan Misra, and many others produce top-tier publications at CVPR, ICCV, and ECCV consistently. The Robotics Institute provides deep infrastructure and cross-disciplinary strength.

Home to Fei-Fei Li (creator of ImageNet), Silvio Savarese, Juan Carlos Niebles, and others. Stanford's influence on the field through ImageNet, scene understanding, and embodied AI is foundational. Consistent top-tier publication record and strong industry connections.

Faculty including Antonio Torralba, Phillip Isola, William Freeman, and Josh Tenenbaum contribute heavily to vision, generative models, and visual cognition. MIT CSAIL is one of the most productive AI labs globally.

BAIR houses vision researchers such as Trevor Darrell, Alexei Efros, Jitendra Malik, and Angjoo Kanazawa. Seminal contributions to deep learning for vision, image synthesis, and 3D understanding. Extremely high citation impact.

Jia Deng (creator of ImageNet alongside Li), Andrew Owens, David Fouhey, and Jason Corso are among strong vision faculty. Michigan has risen significantly in vision output and impact over the past decade.

Faculty including Dhruv Batra, Devi Parikh (now partly at Meta), James Hays, and Judy Hoffman have made Georgia Tech a top vision institution, particularly in visual question answering, embodied AI, and domain adaptation.

Song-Chun Zhu (now at PKU but built the lab), Stefano Soatto, Alan Yuille (now at JHU), and newer faculty have maintained UCLA as a strong vision center with contributions to generative models, scene understanding, and cognitive vision.

Serge Belongie, Bharath Hariharan, Kilian Weinberger, and Noah Snavely (now at Google but affiliated) have contributed strongly to recognition, few-shot learning, and 3D vision. Cornell Tech in NYC adds additional research momentum.
David Forsyth, Svetlana Lazebnik, Alexander Schwing, and Shenlong Wang contribute to a broad vision program. UIUC has a long history in computer vision and maintains strong publication output.
Alan Yuille, René Vidal, and Gregory Hager lead strong programs in vision, medical imaging, and deep learning theory. JHU has particular strength in biological and medical vision applications.

Olga Russakovsky (co-creator of ImageNet large-scale challenge), Jia Deng (moved to Michigan), Felix Heide, and Ryan Adams contribute to fairness in vision, computational imaging, and probabilistic methods. Strong and growing program.

Steve Seitz, Ira Kemelmacher-Shlizerman, Ali Farhadi (now at Apple/UW), and Ranjay Krishna contribute to 3D reconstruction, face modeling, and vision-language. Strong ties to the Allen Institute for AI amplify impact.

Yann LeCun (Chief AI Scientist at Meta, professor at NYU) and Rob Fergus have deep roots here. Lerrel Pinto and other faculty continue strong vision and robotics work. NYU's influence on deep learning for vision is historically enormous.

Kristen Grauman (now partly at Meta AI), Philipp Krähenbühl, and Qixing Huang contribute to video understanding, 3D vision, and egocentric vision. UT Austin has a growing and impactful vision program.

Shree Nayar's long-running CAVE lab has produced foundational work in computational photography and reflectance. Peter Belhumeur and newer faculty maintain Columbia's presence in vision, though the program is somewhat smaller than peers.

Consistently regarded as the top program for computer vision in the US. Home to landmark researchers including Deva Ramanan, Martial Hebert, Abhinav Gupta, and Ishan Misra (affiliated). The Robotics Institute has produced foundational work in object detection, pose estimation, video understanding, and embodied AI. Enormous faculty depth and PhD output.

Home to Antonio Torralba, Phillip Isola, William Freeman (emeritus), and others. Pioneering contributions to scene understanding, image synthesis (pix2pix), dataset creation (Places, ADE20K), and computational photography. Strong ties to industry labs amplify research impact.

Fei-Fei Li's group created ImageNet, which transformed the field. Current faculty include Jiajun Wu, Silvio Savarese, and Juan Carlos Niebles. Strong in 3D vision, scene graphs, embodied AI, and vision-language models. Exceptional industry connections in Silicon Valley.

Jitendra Malik (foundational figure in vision), Alexei Efros (image synthesis, texture), Trevor Darrell, and Angjoo Kanazawa are among the faculty. Contributions span optical flow, segmentation, generative models, and neural radiance fields. BAIR is one of the most productive AI research groups in the world.

Ali Farhadi (YOLO co-creator, now at Apple/UW), Ira Kemelmacher-Shlizerman, Steve Seitz, and Dieter Fox have built a world-class vision program. Strong in real-time detection, face reconstruction, 3D scene understanding, and robotics-vision integration.

James Rehg, Dhruv Batra (now at Meta/GT), Devi Parikh (now at Meta/GT), and Zsolt Kira are key figures. Strong in visual question answering, embodied AI, activity recognition, and vision-language research. Consistently high CVPR/ICCV/ECCV publication counts.

Jason Corso, David Fouhey, Andrew Owens, and Justin Johnson (vision-language, PyTorch contributor) anchor a strong program. Contributions to video understanding, 3D reconstruction, and multimodal learning. Johnson's work on visual reasoning datasets (CLEVR) has been highly influential.

Serge Belongie, Bharath Hariharan, and Kilian Weinberger contribute to recognition, few-shot learning, and metric learning. Cornell Tech in NYC adds a strong applied research dimension. Solid publication record at top venues.

David Forsyth, Svetlana Lazebnik, and Alexander Schwing lead vision research. Historical depth in object recognition and image segmentation. Strong graduate program with consistent presence at CVPR and ICCV.

Olga Russakovsky (ImageNet co-creator, fairness in vision), Felix Heide (computational imaging), and Ryan Adams contribute to a growing and high-impact program. Russakovsky's work on dataset bias and inclusive AI has been particularly influential.

Song-Chun Zhu (now at PKU but built the program), Stefano Soatto, and Alan Yuille (now at Johns Hopkins) established a strong tradition. Current faculty continue work in generative models, 3D understanding, and cognitive vision. Strong industry ties in the LA tech ecosystem.

Alan Yuille, René Vidal, and Gregory Hager lead research in object recognition, adversarial robustness, medical image analysis, and motion understanding. JHU has a long tradition in mathematical foundations of vision and is particularly strong in medical imaging applications.

Yann LeCun (convolutional neural networks pioneer, now primarily at Meta but affiliated), Rob Fergus, and Kyunghyun Cho contribute to a strong program. The Center for Data Science bridges vision and deep learning. LeCun's foundational influence on the entire field is unmatched.

Larry Davis, Rama Chellappa, and Abhinav Shrivastava lead one of the oldest and most established vision labs in the country. Long history of contributions to face recognition, action recognition, and domain adaptation. UMIACS provides strong institutional support.

Kristen Grauman (now primarily at Meta/FAIR but UT-affiliated), Philipp Krähenbühl, and Qixing Huang contribute to video understanding, first-person vision, 3D shape analysis, and efficient detection. Growing program with increasing publication impact.

CMU's Robotics Institute has been a foundational force in computer vision for decades. Researchers such as Takeo Kanade, Martial Hebert, Deva Ramanan, and Abhinav Gupta have produced seminal work in object detection, scene understanding, optical flow, and 3D reconstruction. CMU consistently ranks among the top producers of computer vision PhDs and top-cited vision papers.

Stanford has been central to the deep learning revolution in vision. Fei-Fei Li created ImageNet, which transformed the field. Researchers including Andrej Karpathy, Justin Johnson, and Silvio Savarese have produced highly influential work on image classification, visual question answering, and embodied AI. SAIL and SVL remain top-tier research environments.

MIT has a long history in computer vision dating to the 1960s. Antonio Torralba, William Freeman, Phillip Isola, and others have produced landmark work in scene recognition, generative models (pix2pix, GAN-based methods), and computational photography. MIT CSAIL is one of the largest and most productive AI/vision research labs in the world.

BAIR houses numerous top vision researchers including Trevor Darrell, Jitendra Malik, Alexei Efros, and Angjoo Kanazawa. Contributions include deformable part models, contrastive learning, neural radiance fields (NeRF co-developed here), and generative image synthesis. Berkeley is consistently among the most cited institutions in computer vision.

UW has produced highly influential vision research through faculty such as Ali Farhadi, Dieter Fox, Steve Seitz, and Ira Kemelmacher-Shlizerman. Key contributions include YOLO (object detection), structure from motion, face reconstruction, and vision-language models. UW is a top destination for vision PhD students.

Michigan has strong vision faculty including Jia Deng (who created ImageNet alongside Li), Andrew Owens, David Fouhey, and Jason Corso. Contributions span image recognition benchmarks, self-supervised learning, and video understanding. Michigan is a consistent top-10 producer of vision research.

Georgia Tech has prominent vision researchers including Devi Parikh, Dhruv Batra, James Hays, and Irfan Essa. Work on visual question answering, embodied AI, image geolocalization, and video analysis has been highly cited. The institute has strong ties to industry research labs.

Cornell has contributed significantly through researchers such as Kilian Weinberger, Noah Snavely (3D reconstruction, photo tourism), and Serge Belongie. Work on metric learning, large-scale 3D scene reconstruction, and fine-grained recognition has been influential. Cornell Tech in NYC also bridges academic and applied vision research.

UIUC has a strong vision tradition with researchers including David Forsyth, Derek Hoiem, Alexander Schwing, and Svetlana Lazebnik. Contributions include object recognition, scene understanding, and probabilistic graphical models for vision. UIUC has historically been a top-five vision program.

Princeton hosts influential researchers including Jianxiong Xiao (SUN database), Olga Russakovsky (ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge leadership), and Ryan Adams. Contributions to large-scale benchmarks, 3D scene understanding, and human-in-the-loop learning have been widely recognized.

UCLA's vision research is anchored by Song-Chun Zhu and Alan Yuille, with contributions to compositional models, Bayesian vision, and adversarial robustness. The VCLA group bridges cognitive science and computer vision in distinctive ways.

Columbia's CAVE lab, led by Shree Nayar, has produced foundational work in computational imaging, reflectance modeling, and novel camera designs. Peter Belhumeur and other faculty have contributed to face recognition and biometrics. Columbia has a long and distinguished vision history.

UT Austin has strong vision researchers including Kristen Grauman, whose work on visual similarity, video understanding, and egocentric vision has been highly influential. Philipp Krähenbühl and Qixing Huang also contribute to detection and 3D vision. Grauman's move to Meta AI has not diminished UT's standing.

OSU has a well-established vision program with researchers including Yusu Wang and earlier contributions from Aleix Martinez and Arnab Bhattacharya. The lab has produced solid work in face analysis, action recognition, and medical image analysis, maintaining a consistent presence at top vision venues.

UCSD hosts influential vision researchers including Ravi Ramamoorthi (rendering and reflectance), Manmohan Chandraker, and Xiaolong Wang. Contributions to neural rendering, self-supervised learning, and robotic perception have grown in prominence in recent years.

Consistently ranks #1 in objective metrics like CSRankings for software engineering. CMU has a massive, dedicated institute producing foundational research in software architecture, program analysis, and self-adaptive systems.
Highly influential in software testing, verification, and program analysis, with a strong track record of top-tier publications at ICSE, FSE, and ISSTA.
Renowned for pioneering work in automated debugging, software testing, refactoring, and empirical software engineering.
Maintains a very strong presence in software analysis, testing, maintenance, and program comprehension, consistently publishing at the highest volume in top SE venues.
Leads in formal methods, automated software testing, and the analysis of mobile and concurrent applications.
Has an excellent track record in program analysis, software security, and software engineering, with highly cited faculty in the SE community.
Produces high-impact research in program synthesis, verification, and systems software, often bridging the gap between SE, AI, and systems.
Produces foundational and highly cited work in software design, program analysis, and synthesis, though their focus often leans heavily into programming languages.
Boasts one of the largest software engineering faculties in the US, highly active in empirical software engineering, mining software repositories, and software security.
Strong contributions to empirical software engineering, software quality, and the analysis of open-source software ecosystems.
Has historic and ongoing strength in software architecture, software engineering environments, and human aspects of software engineering.
Conducts innovative research in software engineering for AI, medical devices, autonomous systems, and software testing.
A pioneer in software cost estimation (e.g., COCOMO) and empirical software engineering, maintaining strong ties with the aerospace and defense software industries.
Strong in empirical software engineering, software security, and the intersection of human-computer interaction and software development.
While often more focused on AI and systems, Stanford produces highly influential research in program analysis, bug finding, and software systems.
Consistently ranked at the top for software engineering research, CMU houses pioneers in software architecture, formal methods, and empirical software engineering.
Highly influential in software testing, program analysis, and the human-computer interaction aspects of software engineering.
Produces a massive volume of top-tier research with a strong focus on software testing, refactoring, and automated program repair.
World-renowned for foundational contributions to formal methods, program analysis, and software design.
Leads groundbreaking research in program synthesis, verification, and the intersection of software engineering and machine learning.
Maintains excellent research output in software testing, maintenance, program analysis, and software security.
Known for rigorous research in formal verification, software testing, and model-driven engineering.
Pioneering work in static analysis, bug finding, and the development of scalable software systems.
Historically significant and currently highly active in software architecture, collaborative software engineering, and software environments.
Highly productive in empirical software engineering, software security, and the application of AI to software engineering.
Strong reputation for research in software security, program analysis, and testing methodologies.
Notable for empirical software engineering, software security, and studying human factors in software development.
Leading research in automated program repair, software testing, and mobile application analysis.
Historically foundational in software cost estimation (e.g., COCOMO) and software architecture, maintaining a strong empirical focus.
Strong contributions to software analysis, testing, and self-adaptive software systems.

Consistently ranks at the very top of objective metrics like CSRankings for software engineering. CMU has deep historical ties to the field, including its relationship with the federally funded Software Engineering Institute (SEI), and leads in areas like software architecture, program analysis, and socio-technical ecosystems.

UIUC is a powerhouse in software engineering research, particularly renowned for its pioneering work in automated software testing, refactoring, and program analysis. Its faculty consistently publish at the highest volume in top venues like ICSE, FSE, and ISSTA.

UW is highly influential in program analysis, software verification, and the intersection of software engineering with human-computer interaction. Their researchers have created widely used tools and methodologies for building reliable software.

MIT has a profound historical and ongoing impact on software engineering, particularly in formal methods, software design concepts, and program synthesis. Their work often bridges the gap between theoretical computer science and practical software reliability.

UC Berkeley has produced foundational research in program analysis, software synthesis, and systems engineering. Their research often intersects with programming languages and security, leading to highly cited and widely adopted tools.

UT Austin is a major leader in formal methods, software testing, and automated verification. The ARiSE center brings together top researchers who have significantly advanced the state of the art in making software more reliable and secure.

Georgia Tech has a very strong presence in empirical software engineering, software analysis, and testing. Their researchers are highly active in top-tier conferences and have made significant contributions to understanding developer behavior and software evolution.

UC Irvine is historically significant for its foundational contributions to software architecture (including the REST architectural style). Its ISR continues to be a leading hub for research in software design, testing, and socio-technical aspects of software engineering.

UC Davis is highly prolific in empirical software engineering, open-source software research, and software quality. Their faculty frequently publish highly cited work analyzing large-scale software repositories and developer ecosystems.

Purdue is a leader in the intersection of software engineering and security. Their research excels in program analysis, bug detection, and software testing, making significant impacts on how vulnerabilities are identified and patched.

NCSU has one of the strongest empirical software engineering groups in the country. They are particularly known for their work in software analytics, automated testing, and applying machine learning to software engineering problems.

UMass Amherst has a long-standing reputation for excellence in software engineering, particularly in software analysis, testing, and verification. The LASER group has historically been a major contributor to the field's theoretical and practical foundations.

The University of Michigan has made highly influential contributions to search-based software engineering, automated program repair, and the engineering of medical device software, bridging SE with critical cyber-physical systems.

While Stanford is often broader in its systems and AI research, its specific contributions to software engineering—particularly in program analysis, bug finding (e.g., Coverity's origins), and AI for code—have been massively influential in both academia and industry.

USC is historically vital to the field of software engineering economics and processes, being the birthplace of the COCOMO cost estimation model. It remains a strong institution for research in software systems engineering and architecture.

CMU consistently leads in computer vision publication volume and impact at top conferences (CVPR, ICCV, ECCV). The Robotics Institute houses pioneers in 3D vision, object recognition, and autonomous systems.
MIT CSAIL is renowned for foundational research in scene understanding, computational photography, and the intersection of vision and cognitive science.
Stanford has historically driven the deep learning revolution in vision (e.g., ImageNet) and continues to produce top-tier research in embodied AI, 3D vision, and visual reasoning.
UC Berkeley is a powerhouse in image synthesis, self-supervised learning, and multimodal AI, featuring some of the most highly cited faculty in the history of the field.
UIUC has a deep historical legacy in computer vision and remains highly active in scene understanding, 3D reconstruction, and robust visual learning.
Operating across Ithaca and Cornell Tech in NYC, Cornell excels in fine-grained visual categorization, 3D vision, and the intersection of vision and graphics.
UW is highly influential in computational photography, augmented reality, and computer vision in the wild, often collaborating closely with nearby tech giants.
UCSD has rapidly grown its vision faculty, producing highly impactful work in 3D deep learning, autonomous driving, and embodied AI.
UT Austin is a leader in egocentric (first-person) vision, video understanding, and the intersection of vision and language.
UMD has a long-standing reputation in biometrics, surveillance, and foundational computer vision algorithms, maintaining a strong presence at top conferences.
Georgia Tech produces significant research in embodied AI, visual question answering (VQA), and robotics-oriented computer vision.
Columbia is known for its strong contributions to video prediction, self-supervised learning, and the integration of vision with physical world semantics.
UCLA has a strong tradition in mathematical foundations of vision, computational imaging, and interpretable computer vision models.
Michigan has built a highly productive group focusing on 3D scene understanding, vision and language, and multimodal perception.
Princeton is highly regarded for its work in fairness in AI, visual recognition, and the intersection of computer vision with computer graphics.

CMU consistently ranks at or near the very top for computer vision publication volume at premier conferences (CVPR, ICCV, ECCV). The Robotics Institute is historically one of the largest and most influential centers for vision research in the world.
MIT's CSAIL houses pioneers in computer vision, focusing on scene understanding, computational photography, and the intersection of vision and cognitive science.
Stanford has a massive impact on modern computer vision, notably through the creation of ImageNet. SVL focuses heavily on the intersection of vision, machine learning, and robotics.
UC Berkeley is a powerhouse in deep learning and computer vision, with BAIR producing foundational research in image synthesis, object detection, and autonomous driving.
UIUC has a long-standing, historically significant computer vision group that continues to produce high-impact research in 3D vision, recognition, and multimodal learning.
Cornell (including Cornell Tech in NYC) features leading researchers in fine-grained visual categorization, 3D reconstruction, and the intersection of vision and graphics.
UMD has a very strong tradition in computer vision, particularly in biometrics, visual surveillance, and foundational image processing techniques.
UMich has rapidly expanded its AI and vision faculty, producing top-tier research in video understanding, embodied AI, and 3D computer vision.
UT Austin is highly active in visual recognition, video analysis, and egocentric vision, often collaborating closely with its strong robotics and machine learning groups.
UW is world-renowned for its work at the intersection of computer vision and computer graphics, particularly in 3D reconstruction, computational photography, and AR/VR.
Georgia Tech has a large and productive vision faculty focusing on interactive vision, robotics, and machine learning applications in visual domains.
UCSD excels in statistical visual computing, medical image analysis, and the integration of vision with cognitive science and graphics.
UCLA has a strong theoretical foundation in computer vision, known for work in visual geometry, medical imaging, and robust pattern recognition.
Columbia produces excellent research in computational photography, physics-based vision, and multimodal learning (vision and language).
JHU is highly respected for its rigorous mathematical approaches to computer vision, particularly in medical imaging, generative models, and visual cognition.

Consistently ranks at the very top for publication volume and impact at CVPR, ICCV, and ECCV. CMU has pioneered foundational work in 3D vision, human pose estimation (e.g., OpenPose), and autonomous driving.
A historical and modern powerhouse in computer vision, known for groundbreaking research in scene understanding, computational photography, and the intersection of vision with cognitive science.
Famous for the creation of ImageNet, which catalyzed the deep learning revolution in computer vision. SVL continues to lead in visual relationship detection, embodied AI, and scene graph generation.
Home to foundational modern computer vision architectures, including the R-CNN family for object detection and pioneering work in image-to-image translation (e.g., CycleGAN, pix2pix).
Maintains a massive and highly influential output in top-tier vision conferences, with deep historical roots in object recognition, face recognition, and robust 3D computer vision.
Highly influential in 3D reconstruction, fine-grained visual categorization, and the intersection of vision and graphics, often collaborating closely with Cornell Tech in NYC.
A long-standing leader in the field with significant contributions to biometrics, visual surveillance, domain adaptation, and robust visual reasoning.
Produces highly cited research in active vision, egocentric (first-person) vision, video understanding, and the intersection of vision and language.
